---
title: "Étale cohomology of curves"
date: "2025-04-09"
description: >
   We study the cohomology of algebraic curves.
categories: [Advanced, Algebraic Geometry]
draft: true
draft-mode: unlinked
---


::: {#lem-vanishcohomology}

Let $X$ be a finite scheme over a separably closed field $k$. Then $\mathrm{H}^i(X,\mathcal{F})=0$ for $i>0$ and for all $\mathcal{F}\in \mathrm{Sh}(X_{ét})$.
:::

::: {.proof}

Since the structure map $f:X\to \mathrm{Spec}(k)$ is finite, $f_*:\mathrm{Sh}(X _{ét})\to\mathrm{Sh}(\mathrm{Spec}(k)_{ét})$ is exact. Therefore, the derived functor $\mathrm{R}^qf_*$ vanishes for every $q>0$. But we have the spectral sequence
$$
	\mathrm{E}_2 ^{p,q}=\mathrm{H}^p(\mathrm{Spec}(k) _{ét}, \mathrm{R}^qf_* \mathcal{F})\Longrightarrow \mathrm{H} ^{p+q}(X _{ét}, \mathcal{F})
$$
Hence, by the vanishing of $R^qf_*$ we get that $\mathrm{H}^p(\mathrm{Spec}(k) _{ét}, f_* \mathcal{F})\cong \mathrm{H}^p(X _{ét}, \mathcal{F})$. Since $k$ is separably closed, it is a strictly henselian ring, so global sections are isomorphic to stalks. Hence global sections are exact, and the cohomology vanishes.

:::



::: {#lem-support}

Let $X$ be a scheme, $i: Z\to X$ a closed immersion, and $\mathcal{F}\in \mathrm{Sh}(X_{ét})$ supported on $Z$. Then the canonical morphism $\eta: \mathcal{F}\to i_*i^* \mathcal{F}$ is an isomorphism.
:::
::: {.proof}

The natural morphism $\eta$ is an isomorphism on stalks, indeed
$$
	(i_*i^* \mathcal{F}) _{\bar x}\cong \mathcal{F} _{\bar x}
$$ 
for every geometric point $\bar x$, since $\mathcal{F}$ is supported on $Z$.

:::



::: {#cor-sumskyscraper}

## of @lem-support

Let $X$ be a scheme and $\mathcal{F}\in X_{ét}$ supported on finitely many closed points of $X$. Then 
$$
	\mathcal{F}\cong\bigoplus_{x\in \mathrm{supp}(\mathcal{F})}x_* \mathcal{F} _{x}
$$
:::

::: {.proof}

We apply @lem-support with $Z:=\{x\in X: x \text{ is a geometric point}\}$ and $i: Z\to X$. We then have that 
$$
	\mathcal{F}\cong i_*i^* \mathcal{F}
$$
and
$$
	i_*i^* \mathcal{F}\cong i_*\Big(\bigoplus_{x\in \mathrm{supp}(X)} \mathcal{F}_x\Big) \cong \bigoplus_{x\in \mathrm{supp}(X)} x_*\mathcal{F}_x
$$
where the latter isomorphism holds because the points are finitely many.

:::

::: {#lem-vanishingskyscraper}

Let $X$ be a scheme, $x$ be a geometric point of $X$, and $\mathcal{F}\in \mathrm{Sh}(x _{ét})$. Then for every $i>0$
$$
	\mathrm{R}^ix_* \mathcal{F}=0
$$

In particular,
$$
	\mathrm{H}^i(X _{ét}, x_*\mathcal{F})=0
$$
for all $i>0$.
:::

::: {.proof}

Consider an exact sequence 
$$
	0\to \mathcal{F}\to \mathcal{G}\to \mathcal{H}\to 0
$$ 
of étale sheaves over $x _{ét}$. We have that $x_* \mathcal{G}\to x_* \mathcal{H}$ is surjective, therefore $\mathrm{R}^ix_* \mathcal{F}=0$. As a consequence, with the same reasoning used in the proof of @lem-vanishcohomology using spectral sequence we obtain $\mathrm{H}^i(X _{ét}, \mathcal{F})=0$.

:::



::: {#lem-torsion}

Let $X$ be a quasi-compact quasi-separated (qcqs) scheme, and $\mathcal{F}\in \mathrm{Sh}(X_{ét})$ be a torsion sheaf. Then $\mathrm{H}^i(X, \mathcal{F})$ is a torsion group for all $i\geq 0$.
:::

::: {.proof}

Let $\mathcal{F}[n]$ be the kernel of the multiplication by $n$ map $\mathcal{F}\xrightarrow{\cdot n} \mathcal{F}$. We have an isomorphism
$$
	\mathrm{colim} (\mathcal{F}[n])\to \mathcal{F}
$$
because it is an isomorphism on stalks:
$$
	(\mathrm{colim} \mathcal{F}[n])_x\cong\mathrm{colim}((\mathcal{F}[n])_x)\cong \mathrm{colim}((\mathcal{F}_x)[n])\cong \mathcal{F}_x
$$

Since $X$ is qcqs, the cohomology commutes with colimits:
$$
	\mathrm{H}^i(X _{ét}, \mathcal{F})\cong \mathrm{H}^i(X _{ét}, \mathrm{colim}(\mathcal{F}[n]))\cong \mathrm{colim}(\mathrm{H}^i(X _{ét}, \mathcal{F}[n]))
$$

Since the multiplication by $n$ map is the zero map on $\mathcal{F}[n]$, in cohomology it is again the zero map (being the cohomology functor additive). Therefore, the cohomology is a colimit of torsion groups, ad therefore it is a torsion group.

:::


::: {#lem-c-one-field}

Let $k$ be a separably closed field, $K/k$ a trascendence degree $1$ field extension, and $L/K$ be an algebraic and separable field extension. Then $L':=(L\otimes_k \bar k)_{\mathrm{red}}$, where $\bar k$ denotes an algebraic closure, is a field with $C_1$ property and $L'/L$ is purely inseparable.
:::

::: {.proof}

We first show that $L'/L$ is purely inseparable. If $\operatorname{char}(k)=0$, then $k$ is already algebraically closed, so $\bar k=k$, hence $L'=L$, and there is nothing to prove. If $\mathrm{char}(k)=p>0$, it is easy to verify that for every $x \in L\otimes _k \bar k$ we get for a suitable $n$ that $x^{p^n}\in L$ (write $x$ as sum of elementary tensor). Thus, passing to the reduction give the purely inseparable extension.

It remains to prove that $L'$ is $C_1$. Since $L/k(t)$ is algebraic, $A:=L\otimes_k \bar k$
is integral over $k(t)\otimes_k \bar k$.
Now $t$ is transcendental over $k$, so
$$
k(t)\otimes_k \bar k \cong \bar k(t).
$$
Hence $A$ is integral over the field $\bar k(t)$, and therefore $L/\bar k(t)$ is algebraic.

Now $\bar k$ is algebraically closed, so by Tsen's theorem the field $\bar k(t)$ is $C_1$. Since algebraic extensions of $C_1$-fields are again $C_1$, it follows that $L'$ is $C_1$.
:::


::: {#thm-multiplicative-sheaf}

Let $C$ be an algebraic curve (of dimension 1, finite type, and separated over a field) over a separably closed field $k$. Then
$$
	\mathrm{H}^i(C, \mathcal{O}_{C,ét})=
	\begin{cases}
		\mathcal{O}_C(C)^\times &i=0\\
		\mathrm{Pic}(C) &i=1 \\
		0 &i>1 \text{ and $k$ is algebraically closed}\\
		p^\infty \text{ torsion group } &i>1 \text{ and $k$ is non perfect with char($k$)=p}
	\end{cases}
$$

:::


::: {.proof}

It is clear for $i=0$ and we will not prove the case for $i=1$, it follows from the relation between torsors and $\mathrm{H}^1$.

Let's study the case of $i>1$.

- **Step 1:** suppose the curve to be regular and connected.

  In particular the curve is integral, since every stalk is a noetherian regular local ring (hence reduced) and since every point belongs to exactly one irreducible component. Since irreducible components are closed (by noetherianity), the curve is indeed integral.

  Let $\eta$ be the generic point. Consider the following exact sequence:
  $$
  	0\to \mathcal{O}_{C, \mathrm{ét}}^\times\to \eta_* \mathcal{O}_{\eta, \mathrm{ét}}^\times \to \bigoplus _{c\in C \text{ closed }} c_* \mathbb{Z}\to 0
  $$ {#eq-exact-sequence-regular}
  
  where the second map is the divisor map. This sequence is exact since on stalk at a geometric point $\bar x$ it is given by
  $$
  	0\to (\mathcal{O}_{C, \bar x} ^{\mathrm{sh}})^\times \to (\mathrm{Frac}(\mathcal{O}_{C, \bar x} ^{\mathrm{sh}}))^\times\to \mathbb{Z}\to 0
  $$
  and the right hand side map is the valuation map.
 
  Taking cohomology of sequence [-@eq-exact-sequence-regular] gives that 
  $$
  	\mathrm{H}^i(C, \mathcal{O}_{C, \mathrm{ét}}^\times)\cong \mathrm{H}^i(C, \eta_* \mathcal{O}_{\eta, \mathrm{ét}}^\times)
  $$ {#eq-iso-pushforward}
  for all $i>1$, since by @cor-sumskyscraper the right hand side of the sequence vanishes in cohomology.
  
  We have that for every geometric point $c$ of $C$
  $$
	(\mathrm{R}^q\eta _* \mathcal{O}_{\eta, \mathrm{ét}} ^\times)_c\cong \mathrm{H}^q((\eta\times_X \mathrm{Spec}(\mathcal{O}_{C,c} ^{\mathrm{sh}}))_{ét}, (\mathcal{O}^\times _{\eta,\mathrm{ét}})_{|\eta\times_X \mathrm{Spec}(O _{C,c} ^{\mathrm{sh}})})	
  $$ {#eq-iso-stalk-higher-image}

  
  $\eta \times \mathrm{Spec}(\mathcal{O} _{C,c}^\mathrm{sh})=\mathrm{Spec}(\mathrm{Frac}(\mathcal{O}_{C,c}^\mathrm{sh}))$, write $L$ for $\mathrm{Frac}(\mathcal{O}_{C,c} ^{\mathrm{sh}})$ so that the restriction of $\mathcal{O}_{\eta ,\mathrm{ét}}^\times$ appearing in right hand side of the claim is just $\mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Spec}(L),\mathrm{ét}}^\times$. Observe that $L$ is an algebraic separable extension of the function field $k(C)$. Indeed $O _{C,c}^ \mathrm{sh}$ is the filtered colimit of local étale $\mathcal{O _{C,c}}$-algebras $B$, and for each such $B$ the field $\mathrm{Frac}(B)=B\otimes _{\mathcal{O}_{C,c}}k(C)$ is a finite separable extension of $k(C)$, thus every element of $L$ lies in some finite separable extension of $k(C)$. By the relation between Galois and étale cohomology we have
  $$
  	\mathrm{H}^q(\mathrm{Spec}(L), \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Spec}(L), \mathrm{ét}}^\times)\cong \mathrm{H}^q(\mathrm{Gal}(L^\mathrm{sep}/L), (L^{\mathrm{sep}})^\times)
  $$ {#eq-iso-gal-etale}
  
  By Hilbert 90 this vanishes for $q=1$. 

  ::: {.claim #clm-p-torsion}
  **Claim.**
  The group
  $$
    \mathrm{H}^q(\mathrm{Gal}(L^\mathrm{sep}/L), (L^{\mathrm{sep}})^\times)
  $$
  is a $p^\infty$-torsion group for every $q\ge 2$.
  :::
  
  
  ::: {.proof}
  
  By @lem-c-one-field, $L':=(L\otimes_k \bar k)_{\mathrm{red}}$ is $C_1$-field with $L'/L$ purely inseparable. 
  It is a well known galois cohomology fact that for a $C_1$-field
  $$
  	\mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{Gal}({L'}^{\mathrm{sep}}/L'), ({L'} ^{\mathrm{sep}})^\times)=0
  $$
  for every $i>0$. Therefore, by purely inseparability of $L'/L$ we get (by iso on galois groups)
  $$
  	\mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{Gal}(L ^{\mathrm{sep}}/L), ({L'} ^{\mathrm{sep}})^\times)\cong \mathrm{H}^i(\mathrm{Gal}({L'} ^{\mathrm{sep}}/L'), ({L'} ^{\mathrm{sep}})^\times)=0
  $$
  for every $i>0$.

  Consider the short exact sequence
  $$
  	0\to (L ^{\mathrm{sep}})^\times \to ({L'} ^{\mathrm{sep}})^\times\to Q \to 0
  $$
  where $Q$ is the cokernel of the first map. By pure inseparability of ${L'} ^{\mathrm{sep}}/(L ^{\mathrm{sep}})$ we have that $Q$ is $p^\infty$-torsion group. Passing to cohomology, we get 
  $$
  	\mathrm{H}^{i+1}(\mathrm{Gal}(L ^{\mathrm{sep}}/L), (L ^{\mathrm{sep}})^\times)\cong \mathrm{H} ^{i}(\mathrm{Gal}(L ^{\mathrm{sep}}/L), Q)
  $$
  for every $i>0$. Therefore, by @lem-torsion, we get the claim.
  :::
  
  By isomorphisms [-@eq-iso-stalk-higher-image], [-@eq-iso-gal-etale], and [Claim](#clm-p-torsion) we get that 
  $$
  	(\mathrm{R}^q\eta_* \mathcal{O}_{\eta, \mathrm{ét}}^\times)_c
  $$
  is a $p^\infty$-torsion group at every geometric point $c$, thus the sheaf $R^q \eta_* \mathcal{O}_{\eta , \mathrm{ét}}^\times$ is a $p^\infty$-torsion sheaf. 

  <!--
  Consider now the Grothendieck spectral sequence
  $$
  \mathrm{E} ^{i,j}_2:=\mathrm{H}^i(C _{ét}, \mathrm{R}^j\eta_* \mathcal{O}_{\eta , \mathrm{ét}}^\times)\Longrightarrow \mathrm{H} ^{i+j}(\eta _{\mathrm{ét}}, \mathcal{O}_{\eta , \mathrm{ét}}^\times )
  $$

  For every $i\in \mathbb{Z}$, $\mathrm{E} ^{i,1}_2=0$, by Hilbert 90, since $\mathrm{R}^1\eta_* \mathcal{O}_{\eta , \mathrm{ét}}^\times=0$ by isomorphisms [-@eq-iso-stalk-higher-image] and [-@eq-iso-gal-etale]. For every $i\geq 2$, we proved that $\mathrm{R}^j\eta _* \mathcal{O}_{\eta , \mathrm{ét}}^\times$ is a $p^\infty$-torsion sheaf, and by @lem-torsion, we get that $\mathrm{E} ^{i,j}_2$ is a $p^\infty$-torsion abelian group. Therefore,
  $$
  	\mathrm{E} ^{i,j}_2\otimes _{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[p ^{-1}]=0
  $$ 
  for every $j\geq 1$ 
  -->

  Consider the Grothendieck spectral sequence
  $$
    \mathrm{E}_2^{i,j}:=\mathrm{H}^i(C_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\mathrm{R}^j\eta_*\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}})
    \Longrightarrow
    \mathrm{H}^{i+j}(\eta_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}}).
  $$
  Since this is a first-quadrant spectral sequence, we also get
  $$
    \mathrm{E}_2^{i,j}\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}[p^{-1}]
    \Longrightarrow
    \mathrm{H}^{i+j}(\eta_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}})\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}[p^{-1}].
  $$

  For every $j\geq 1$, we proved that $\mathrm{R}^j\eta_*\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}}$ is a $p^\infty$-torsion sheaf. Hence $\mathrm{E}_2^{i,j}=\mathrm{H}^i(C_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\mathrm{R}^j\eta_*\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}})$ is a $p^\infty$-torsion group for every $i\geq 0$ and every $j\geq 1$. Therefore
  $$
    \mathrm{E}_2^{i,j}\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}[p^{-1}]=0
    \qquad (j\geq 1).
  $$
  Thus the localized spectral sequence has only the row $j=0$ possibly nonzero, and therefore it degenerates at the $\mathrm{E}_2$-page. Hence for every $n>0$ one gets an isomorphism
  $$
    \mathrm{E}_2^{n,0}\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}[p^{-1}]
    \cong
    \mathrm{H}^n(\eta_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}})\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}[p^{-1}].
  $$

  Now $\mathrm{H}^1(\eta_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}})=0$ by Hilbert-90, and for every $n\geq 2$ the group $\mathrm{H}^n(\eta_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}})$ is a $p^\infty$-torsion group by the same Galois-cohomological argument as above, applied to the field $k(C)$. Therefore
  $$
    \mathrm{H}^n(\eta_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}})\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}[p^{-1}]=0
    \qquad (n>0).
  $$
  It follows that
  $$
    \mathrm{E}_2^{n,0}\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}[p^{-1}]=0
    \qquad (n>1).
  $$

  Since $\mathrm{E}_2^{n,0}=\mathrm{H}^n(C_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\eta_*\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}})$, we obtain
  $$
    \mathrm{H}^n(C_{\mathrm{\acute et}},\eta_*\mathcal O^\times_{\eta,\mathrm{\acute et}})\otimes_{\mathbb{Z}}\mathbb{Z}[p^{-1}]=0
    \qquad (n>1).
  $$
  
  By isomorphism [-@eq-iso-pushforward], this gives the claim in the case of a regular connected curve.

- **Step 2:** the curve is regular

    Apply **Step 1** to every connected component.

- **Step 3:** the curve is reduced
:::

::: {#thm-smooth-proper-unity}
## Theorem Name

Content
:::
